name: Big O Notation: Comparison of Computational Growth Rates
description:
Big O notation is used in Computer Science to describe the performance or complexity of an algorithm. It specifically describes the worst-case scenario, and can be used to describe the execution time required or the space used by an algorithm.

  • O(1) - Constant Time: The execution time remains the same regardless of input size.
  • O(log n) - Logarithmic Time: Complexity increases very slowly (e.g., Binary Search).
  • O(n) - Linear Time: Complexity increases proportionately to the input size (e.g., iterating through a list).
  • O(n log n) - Linearithmic Time: Common in efficient sorting algorithms like Merge Sort or Quick Sort.
  • O(n²) - Quadratic Time: Performance is proportional to the square of the input size (e.g., nested loops).
  • O(2ⁿ) - Exponential Time: Growth doubles with each addition to the input data set, becoming impractical very quickly.
dataGenerationCode: const nValues = []; for (let i = 1; i <= 25; i += 0.5) { nValues.push(i); } const traces = [ { x: nValues, y: nValues.map(() => 1), name: 'O(1)', line: { color: '#10b981', width: 3 } }, { x: nValues, y: nValues.map(n => Math.log2(n)), name: 'O(log n)', line: { color: '#3b82f6', width: 3 } }, { x: nValues, y: nValues.map(n => n), name: 'O(n)', line: { color: '#6366f1', width: 3 } }, { x: nValues, y: nValues.map(n => n * Math.log2(n)), name: 'O(n log n)', line: { color: '#8b5cf6', width: 3 } }, { x: nValues, y: nValues.map(n => Math.pow(n, 2)), name: 'O(n²)', line: { color: '#f59e0b', width: 3 } }, { x: nValues, y: nValues.map(n => Math.pow(2, n)), name: 'O(2ⁿ)', line: { color: '#ef4444', width: 3 } } ]; return traces.map(t => ({ ...t, type: 'scatter', mode: 'lines', hoverinfo: 'y+name' }));
